data.title: CAVA Group: New Initiatives Will Reap Long-Term Benefits (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I first reviewed CAVA Group, Inc. (CAVA) in early 2024. While I thought this founder-led business was making impressive strides forward, I ultimately gave the company a “Hold” rating because I thought CAVA’s valuation was outrageous.

A year and some change later, like many restaurants and fast casual chains, CAVA has fallen on some hard times. While the S&P 500 is up by over 15% YTD, CAVA’s stock price has fallen by over 50%.

Despite these macro factors at play, I’m a believer in the long-term prospects of this company and I’ll share with investors while this may be an opportune time to buy shares of this quality business. Let’s dive in!

Expansion Continues Yet Macro Risks Endure

CAVA continues their expansion plans as the business as of Q3 2025 had 415 restaurants and management expects to open between 68 to 70 net new locations in 2025. On the Q3 earnings call, management noted they expect to grow unit count by at least 16%. When speaking about new stores here is what CFO Tricia Tolivar had to say, “Looking ahead, we remain confident in the long-term structural health of the business reaffirmed by our strong AUVs and new restaurant performance. Our most recent 2025 cohort is trending above $3 million in AUV, with new unit productivity continuing to exceed 100%.”

These stores seem to be performing well yet the business is facing numerous challenges, mostly notably from the macro environment. In prior articles, I’ve described some challenges faced by new and old players in the fast casual space such as employees bringing their lunch to work more often and looking for value oriented deals such as the “3 for Me” deal at Chili’s (EAT).

Furthermore, in my recent article about Sweetgreen (SG) I noted the challenges faced by the younger generations, more specifically Millennials and Gen Z who would frequently patronize Sweetgreen. CAVA’s CEO Brett Schulman noted some of these struggles facing these patrons and how they don’t have the money to eat out as often saying, “So we don't want to overstate the challenges of the consumer, but you can look at the data. They're clearly out there, whether it's student loan repayment, consumer sentiment, just the inflationary pressures all around them, whether it's health care cost, housing costs, right? Gen Z unemployment twice the national average. When we look at the data, it's more that the younger cohort that 25 to 35 that Tricia noted in comments is that they don't have the steam that they had last year in the way that they were visiting or their frequency of visiting.”

Now why there are notable issues with the macro impacting various restaurant companies I don’t think it’s fair to say Sweetgreen and CAVA are struggling in a similar fashion. Both are down significantly year-to-date but I believe CAVA is in a much better position to succeed in the long term and I’ll tell you why.

Long-Term Focus Through New Initiatives and Offerings

The first reason I’m inclined to believe CAVA will succeed is that the consistent message you hear from Schulman and the CAVA team is their focus on consistency and delivering value to consumers and I’d argue investors alike. Unlike Sweetgreen who has numerous operational issues and is considered a less favorable place to work based on Glassdoor ratings, CAVA seems to have a far more stable environment and is in a better position to train and promote their employees. Recently, the company launched a program called, Flavor Your Feature, which is a new assistant general manager program that seeks to train employees and set them up for future leadership opportunities. This seems like a very positive initiative and having more knowledgeable, capable employees, this should in turn result in better run operations.

In the restaurant space it’s also important to come up with fresh offerings and CAVA is doing that as well. The company recently launched new salmon offerings and Schulman noted that so far the results from markets testing these offerings was “encouraging.” CAVA also launched cinnamon and sugar pita chips which seem to be popular with patrons as well.

CAVA is also tweaking their rewards program as they are launching tiered status levels. The company launched their rewards program just over a year ago and thus far results seem promising as the program has grown by 36%. Here is what Schulman had to say about this revamped rewards program on the company’s Q3 earnings call, “Through our new sea, sand and sun structure, members now enjoy differentiated benefits and surprise and delight experiences designed to celebrate their loyalty and strengthen long-term engagements. As an extension of our brand spirit of generosity, we also launched status matching to welcome new members and encourage deeper participation. While status matching is a first of its kind offering in our industry, we see it simply as another way to express our concept essence. The latest evolution of our program also includes an expanded rewards catalog with seasonal offerings and fresh new ways to engage.”

While new food offerings or new initiatives won’t move the needle enough to alleviate macro pressures, if CAVA continues to place their focus on key aspects of the business within their control such as creating a quality guest experience and providing training to employees they I believe CAVA will be in a winning position once these macro pressures while ease up.

Financials

In Q3 2025, CAVA generated revenue of roughly $290 million which is a 20% increase compared to Q3 2024. Same sale growth for the quarter was a lowly 1.9%. As you can see from the graphic below, while revenue has increased from Q3 2024, CAVA’s quarterly same store sales growth has been steadily declining quarter after quarter:

CAVA’s restaurant-level profit margin was 24.6% for the quarter which represents growth of 15.1% since Q3 2024. The company opened 17 new restaurants in the quarter bringing the company’s store count up to 415 as you can see below:

While revenue has grown, restaurant operating costs have outpaced this growth as you can see below with 2025 food, beverage, packaging, labor, occupancy and other operating expenses outpacing 2024 costs for both the three and nine month periods as you can see below:

After sharing Q3 results management updated their guidance for the rest of fiscal year 2025 as they updated new restaurant openings while lowering their same-store-sales growth, restaurant level profit margin range and estimated adjusted EBITDA figure as you can see below:

CAVA has maintained an excellent balance sheet as you can see below with enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities and a shrinking accumulated deficit:

Valuation

CAVA has a valuation grade of a “D-” at Seeking Alpha:

As CAVA is still in its growth phase, I believe the best valuation metric for this business is EV/Sales or Price-to-Sales. While CAVA has essentially always traded at a premium since going public in 2023, the company's forward P/S has come down significantly as you can see below. At the beginning of the year CAVA and Wingstop (WING) were still trading much higher than peers Dutch Bros (BROS) and Chipotle (CMG), while now CAVA is much more aligned with those two fellow growth restaurants stocks:

While again, this valuation is still arguably expensive compared to established industry peers, I believe a premium is warranted in CAVA’s case as analysts expect CAVA to continue to grow revenue by over 20% annually over the next several years.

Furthermore, from a ratings perspective both Seeking Alpha and Wall Street analysts are predicting upside for the stock as CAVA has an average price target of $67.63 as you can see below:

Conclusion

CAVA is still facing macro pressures like many of their peers, the business is still seeing revenue growth as well as positive same-store-sales growth while this metric is turning negative for others like Sweetgreen and Chipotle.

CAVA is doing all the right things the business can control, like creating new menu items, improving their rewards program, and establishing a new training initiative for employees. These are all steps to ensure the long-term success of the business.

While CAVA’s stock price may be considered pricey to many others in the restaurant industry, I believe it’s warranted as this founder-led business has continued to grow revenue and delight guests since going public a few years ago.

I believe patient long-term investors can buy shares at these prices and will be rewarded when the overall macro picture improves.

data.title: AGNC Investment: A Fat 13% Dividend Yield, But I'm Not A Buyer
data.description:
AGNC Investment's (AGNC) mix of high leverage and agency-guaranteed residential mortgage-backed securities ("Agency RMBS") has helped mold the security into a formidable source of income with a fat double-digit dividend yield and monthly distributions. The internally managed mREIT last declared a monthly cash dividend of $0.12 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1.44 per share annualized for a 13.28% dividend yield. AGNC's business is built around using leverage to purchase agency RMBS to earn a spread between its costs of funds and the income from an asset best described as a security backed by a pool of residential mortgages whose principal and interest payments are guaranteed by Ginnie Mae or a government-sponsored enterprise (GSEs) like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.

The mREIT's portfolio was valued at $90.8 billion at the end of its fiscal 2025 third quarter, growing sequentially by $8.5 billion, or 10.3%, over the second quarter. This came with an average asset yield of 4.95%, an 8 basis point expansion from the second quarter. AGNC's leverage remained unchanged sequentially at 7.6x. The mREIT generated net interest income of $148 million in the third quarter, a sequential dip of 8.6% from $162 million in the second quarter. This was with a net spread and dollar roll income of $0.35 per share that fell by 3 cents over the same time frame.

Bears would be right to highlight that the mREIT has continually issued new shares, with its weighted average number of common shares outstanding ending the third quarter at 1.056 billion, up significantly over its year-ago figure of 810 million. The mREIT's shares outstanding are up a material 99% over the last 5 years, an annual rate of dilution of around 20%. This is not free money against AGNC's double-digit dividend yield on its common shares, but has been done when the stock price trades at a premium to tangible book value, which should be accretive. However, this strategy of continually expanding its share count even against the negative price performance of its common shares is why the common shares remain an avoid for me. AGNC recorded an annualized third-quarter net interest spread of 1.78%, down 23 basis points from 2.01% in the second quarter.

AGNC's Preferreds Stack And The Series G

The mREIT has six publicly listed preferred securities that are a mix of fixed-to-floating rate and a single fixed (AGNCZ). The earliest issued preferred in the stack was the fixed-to-floating rate Series C (AGNCN), which floated on 10/15/2022 and offered the highest yield as of AGNC's third-quarter earnings report on the 20th of October 2025. The security is floating at 3-month CME Term SOFR plus 5.111% and an adjustment rate of 0.26161%; this is for a total headline coupon of around 9.06% with SOFR at 3.689%.

AGNC's 7.75% Series G Preferreds (AGNCL) offer a headline coupon, which at 7.75% is the second largest in AGNC's preferred equity stack. These currently pay out a $1.9375 annual coupon in quarterly installments for a current yield of 7.74%, with the Series G swapping hands for $24.95 per share. This is a marginal 5 cent discount to their $25 liquidation value per share. This security was issued back in the summer of 2022, when AGNC sold 6,000,000 shares for a total of $150 million before the underwriting discount and other expenses. The same number of shares remain outstanding.

Critically, with the U.S. Treasury yield more rigid than SOFR in terms of mirroring recent rate cuts, the Series G could have a marginally higher floating rate component than the Series C if this dynamic remains constant up until they float at their call date on 10/15/2027. This will be at a rate equal to the sum of the five-year U.S. Treasury Rate plus 4.39%. The U.S. 5-year Bond Yield (US5Y) is at 3.72%, which would place the aggregate headline coupon on the Series G at 8.11%. The Series C overall offers a positive spread on the Series G due to the higher 5.111% floor and adjustment rate, but trades at a 2.8% premium to liquidation value at $25.70 per share.

Conclusion

While AGNC shareholders are currently able to lock in a double-digit level of income from a covered yield that offers an extremely strong spread over the U.S. 10-year Treasury rate, the mREIT's historical price performance has not been great. AGNC reported a tangible net book value of $8.28 per share at the end of the third quarter, a positive improvement from $7.81 per share in the second quarter, with this figure being boosted by a 10.6% economic return during the quarter. The current 31% premium of the common shares over book value also represents a reason for a pause, as it has expanded to its highest level in 5 years. I am rating AGNC and its preferreds as a hold.

data.title: Angi: Great Value Amid Business Transition
data.description:
2025 is quickly drawing to a close, and many investors are nervous about protecting their massive gains next year. My core strategy for doing so is to be unsentimental about selling off winners in my portfolio to protect from downside, while also rotating more of my portfolio to undervalued small and mid-cap rebound plays.

Angi (ANGI) is one of these stocks. The home services marketplace has fallen ~20% over the past year, and remains substantially down from highs above $100 that the company briefly notched during COVID, when demand for home improvements peaked. Despite recent shaky growth trends, I think now is a great time to assess the bull case for Angi on the dip.

I’m initiating Angi at a buy rating. In my view, this is a great way to gain exposure to a very cheap small-cap stock that is already activating several catalysts to get its growth back on track.

Prioritizing homeowner choice on its services marketplace

Angi is a business in the midst of a major transition. Before discussing its major growth initiatives, let’s first get a lay of the land on what Angi does and how it generates revenue.

The snapshot below is taken from Angi’s website. As shown below, the company connects homeowners to a wide range of home services professionals.

The company was founded prior to the dotcom boom in 1995 as “Angie’s List,” initially envisioned as an online yellow pages for trusted reviews on home services pros. In 2017, the company was acquired by the internet conglomerate IAC and bundled with its other home improvement holding, HomeAdvisor. The merged company was rebranded as “Angi.” Earlier this year in May, IAC fully spun off its holdings in Angi, leaving the company to be fully independent.

The company essentially generates revenue as a cut of service requests. Service pros can pay for leads on the platform, or also pay a cut to Angi from their project total when a consumer/homeowner selects them using the Angi portal. The company is a critical marketing platform for home services pros to gain visibility and engage directly with homeowners. Angi operates an array of brands, including Angie’s List and HomeAdvisor alongside Handy in the U.S. Overseas, the company also has a presence through MyBuilder (UK), MyHammer (Germany) and HomeStars (Canada). We do note that the market is very competitive in the U.S., with Angi competing against Yelp’s (YELP) Services segment as well as the likes of Thumbtack and Houzz.

Here’s the tough part: Angi is currently a business in decline. Revenue is declining -10% y/y, while leads and service pros on the platform are also in decline. The chart below showcases the company’s core business metrics. Total service requests fell -8% y/y to 4.14 million in the most recent quarter. Also, the number of qualified pros using the platform fell -17% y/y to an average of 131k in Q3.

That said, we note that the company has kicked off major strategic changes this year. Since January, the company is focusing its efforts on “homeowner choice.” What this means is that inbound leads on Angi will no longer be automatically matched with available service pros upon filling out a request. Homeowners will be shown an array of profiles, and select (or decline) the service pros of their choice. Angi is also deprioritizing traffic and leads from third party sites (which is what it calls its “network” business), and focusing on building traffic from its first-party brands.

This is the major catalyst behind Angi’s decline in leads and revenue, as it has generated the expected reduction in leads. That said, lead quality is improving. The company noted in Q3 that its hire rate (the percentage of homeowners who hire through Angi after placing a service request) has increased roughly 10 points y/y. The win rate for pros has increased 30%, boosting the marketing value of Angi for its base of pros: which, in turn, has also improved churn rates for its pros by 7%.

The other reasons for the decline are macro-driven. Amid macroeconomic concern and a stagnant housing market, fewer people are pursuing housing projects - so this headwind is also playing out in the data for other publicly traded home services competitors like Yelp. Also, the company is facing SEO headwinds from lower Google searches. Part of this issue may be secular as more traffic moves to AI chatbots. We note that in November, after the close of Angi’s most recent quarter, the company launched its plugin for ChatGPT, which may help to improve organic traffic.

In my view, Angi’s shift in focus toward consumer choice, which is also improving net promoter scores for the platform, will yield long term payoffs despite the near term headwinds the company is trading off for it.

Profitability is rising, and valuation is opportunistic

What we do also appreciate about Angi is that in the wake of top line headwinds, the company is striving to cut costs wherever possible.

As shown in the chart below, Angi grew its adjusted EBITDA at 12% y/y to $39.7 million, reflecting a 14.9% margin that improved 300bps y/y versus an 11.9% margin in the year-ago period.

The company is focused heavily on improving the ROO from its traffic. It is also expecting further margin expansion to play out next year, per CEO Jeffrey Kip’s remarks on the Q&A portion of the Q3 earnings call:

And we think our significantly improved customer experience and the solid ROI there is, I think, attributing to that.

I think revenue growth rates will likely vary through the year, likely a little lower in the first half of the year as we compare to higher network service request volume, and evening out over the course of the year. I think it's also just worth mentioning what we said on the last call that we expect a little leverage from revenue to EBITDA growth as we keep our strong fixed cost discipline next year.”

Now we come to the crux of my buy rating: With Angi’s profitability expanding despite a collapsing share price, the stock’s valuation multiples have dropped too low for us to ignore.

At current share prices near $13, Angi trades at a market cap of $561.4 million. Netting off the $340.7 million of cash and $497.5 million of debt on the company’s latest balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $718.4 million.

For next year FY26, Wall Street is expecting Angi to hit $1.07 billion in revenue, or 4% y/y growth. If we conservatively assume flat adjusted EBITDA margins of 14.9% (flat to YTD actuals, notwithstanding the company’s commentary that it should see adjusted EBITDA growth in excess of revenue next year), adjusted EBITDA would be $159.4 million. This positions Angi’s valuation at 4.5x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.

Needless to say, this is an incredibly low valuation multiple in a very expensive stock market.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are risks that we need to be careful of here. As previously noted, Angi competes in a very competitive landscape for what appears to be, at least in the near term, a shrinking pie of demand for home services. Recent churn in service pros and the decline in leads is also a core metric that we need to keep an eye on, and ensure that the company does indeed return to net pro and leads growth after its homeowner choice switchover this year.

That said, I consider Angi’s low valuation multiples to price in a considerable amount of risk. While I wouldn’t bet the proverbial farm on this stock, Angi is worth a small and measured buy.

data.title: TeraWulf Vs Cipher Mining: Winners In 2026's AI Acceleration Story
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Next year is going to be a make-or-break year for crypto miners.

The stagnation in Bitcoin’s price so far has cornered crypto miners into a difficult position of winding down operations of their GPU infrastructure while demonstrating a return on these compute assets at the same time.

With the colossal proliferation of AI and HPC workloads, crypto miners have figured out that pivoting to AI will give them a 2x benefit—keeping the lights on in their GPU data centers while also raising their margin profiles. Even better, many crypto miners in 2025 scored massive deals with the big spenders, hyperscalers, and frontier LLM labs. All eyes turn to how crypto miners eventually deliver on these big deals in 2026—the year of AI reckoning for these crypto miners.

TeraWulf (WULF) and Cipher Mining (CIFR) are two such crypto miners who also jumped on the crypto-to-AI pivot bandwagon in 2025. I had previously recommended a Buy rating on both these companies, and I continue to recommend a Buy rating on both of them.

TeraWulf and Cipher Mining’s Unique Pivot To AI

In the past couple of months, I initiated Buy ratings on TeraWulf and Cipher Mining, citing impressive dynamics engineered in their respective HPC deals. Unfortunately, markets soured on prospects of AI tailwinds since then, with many investors questioning the tenacity of these tailwinds. My view stands firm—that AI is a strong secular force that will positively impact those that invest with AI in mind, and the recent pullbacks in the market give valuations a much-needed respite from the excitement in AI.

2025 has definitely shown the appreciation markets have towards the changing nature of crypto miners’ pivot towards supporting global AI workloads. The operations of all crypto miners typically centered on working towards securing three main resources—GPU, data center infrastructure, and power to support their crypto mining operations. But the severe downturn in crypto this year, as seen in the price of Bitcoin, the mothership of all cryptocurrencies, has complicated the business model of crypto miners. This can be seen in the declining hash rate index, a closely watched yardstick for the crypto mining industry’s revenues.

A declining hash rate index should have been alarming for the crypto mining industry because it indicates stress in the business environment for crypto miners along with risks to profitability. But in 2025, we also saw a strong divergence where stocks of crypto miner companies decoupled from Bitcoin (BTC-USD), as seen below.

With the volume of AI and HPC workloads surging, demand for compute has also skyrocketed. So far, hyperscalers had attempted to keep up with their need for compute by raising their capex budgets well beyond 50-60% CAGR rates.

But the intense scrutiny on the free cash flows of hyperscalers seen in the past 2-3 months will have surely put a lid on the days of 50-60% capex CAGR that is expected to slow down to just 25% by the end of this year. Faced with this conundrum of slowing capex along with rising compute needs, hyperscalers are increasingly going to crypto miners, who are in prime position to quickly scale data center capacity because most of the crypto miners already have contracts in place with their respective power companies and infrastructure providers to bring data center compute online.

Of all the crypto miners who have been racing to score deals where they offer up their compute and power resources to hyperscalers in exchange for annualized subscriptions, TeraWulf and Cipher Mining stand out in the crypto miner complex. Both TeraWulf and Cipher Mining have only one major client in common, Alphabet’s Google Cloud via Fluidstack, which is not just a customer of TeraWulf and Cipher’s data center infrastructure. Google is also an investor in both these companies, clearly announcing its vested interest in TeraWulf and Cipher Mining.

In TeraWulf’s case, Alphabet will lease >200 MW of critical compute capacity via Fluidstack, which is expected to bring in ~$10B in revenues over the next decade. TeraWulf recently secured all the necessary financing to help them start building out the capacity for Google and Fluidstack, putting them on track for capacity delivery by H2 2026. One of the reasons TeraWulf may have been successful in favorably securing financing to support the build-out is because Google is backstopping their partnership with TeraWulf. The backstop implies Google Cloud is guaranteeing TeraWulf a total of ~$3.2B in lease obligations. The backstop, along with Google’s ~14% stake in TeraWulf (as part of the deal), has helped the crypto miner in securing project finance.

Cipher Mining’s partnership between Google Cloud and Fluidstack is almost the same, except Cipher Mining is offering up a total of 207 MW of capacity by January 2027 to Google/Fluidstack. In exchange, Alphabet is also investing 5.4% in Cipher Mining while backstopping the partnership by ~$1.73B. Analysts were expecting the Cipher’s HPC revenue to come in at ~$127M in 2026, significantly rising to ~$740M in 2027.

That was before Cipher Mining announced another hyperscaler win with Amazon's (AMZN) AWS to deliver ~300 MW of capacity. The total deal is worth ~$5.5B over 15 years.

As it now stands, Wall Street is projecting a 66% jump in Cipher’s 2026 revenues, while TeraWulf’s revenues are expected to rise 132%.

Valuation Levels For Cipher and TeraWulf

If investors look at both TeraWulf and Cipher Mining, they will see that both these companies are trading at forward revenue multiples of ~13-14x. On the face of it, it appears that TeraWulf would look more attractive due to TeraWulf’s 132% projections in 2026 revenues.

However, I believe that Cipher’s revenue expectations are not accurately capturing the company’s ~300 MW agreement with Amazon’s AWS. I estimate the AWS deal can add at least $50M, if not more, to Cipher’s HPC revenues, pushing up the growth rates in Cipher’s 2026 revenues. Previously, I had explained why I believe Cipher’s shares warrant a forward revenue multiple of ~21-22x. That provides scope for Cipher’s revenue multiple to expand by ~45% from current 2026 revenue multiples of 14.5x, implying ~45% upside in Cipher’s shares.

For TeraWulf, we can assume a similar expansion in its multiples if we compare TeraWulf vs Cipher Mining. That does provide scope for revenue multiple expansion in TeraWulf from 13.5 to ~21-22x, implying upside of ~50% in TeraWulf’s shares.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

However, in TeraWulf’s case, investors must note the risk of TeraWulf’s capital structure as compared to Cipher Mining. TeraWulf has ~$711M in cash vs. $1.1B in debt. Compared to TeraWulf, Cipher Mining boasts $1.2B in cash and $1B in debt. This means Cipher trades at an impressive multiple to its book value of just 8x, while TeraWulf richly trades at a book value multiple of 21x.

At a broader level, 2026 will also be the year that will test the level of commitments of crypto miners. Many crypto miners have moved quickly to secure agreements with hyperscalers in the last few months and are quickly working to honor these commitments to bring only many megawatts of compute infrastructure. If these commitments are not honored, crypto miners risk losing millions of dollars' worth of revenues as well as losing the credibility of their business plan to pivot to AI.

Takeaway

2026 will be a crucial year for crypto mining companies, which have used 2025 to pivot to AI and HPC workloads. TeraWulf and Cipher Mining stand out as two mining companies that are well positioned to benefit from the industry pivot towards AI.

I am reiterating my Buy rating on both TeraWulf and Cipher Mining and updating my PT above.

Prediction: 2.6 – 5.3% increase to $1.56 - $1.60

WEC Energy Group (WEC) – 23 years

Prediction: 6.4 – 7.5% increase to $3.80 - $3.84

Actual: 6.7% increase to $3.81

Forward yield: 3.62%

The Milwaukee, Wisconsin-based electric utility continues to compound its payout at 6 – 7% annually.

Predictions for Dividend Increases in January

There are 16 long-term dividend growth companies that I expect to announce annual increases in January. First, here is my prediction for two featured companies:

Chevron Corporation (CVX) – 37 years of dividend growth

The 2nd largest oil & gas company by market cap (only Exxon Mobil is larger), Chevron grew significantly this year when it closed on its acquisition of Hess Corporation. The company’s had a rough couple of years since the price of oil peaked in 2022. The company’s business is broken into two reportable segments: Upstream and Downstream. In the energy industry, Upstream refers to exploration and production of oil and gas fields, while Downstream refers to the refining of raw petroleum products into products usable by the end customer – for example, gasoline, heating oil, and lubricants. Chevron’s Upstream revenues are tied to the price of petroleum products while its Downstream revenues are tied to the margins on the refining processes. The price of oil peaked above $120 per barrel in the first half of 2022 and since then has gradually come down to its current price of $60, meaning that the company’s revenues have taken a hit since peaking when oil prices peaked. From a peak of $18.28 in 2022, earnings per share fell to $9.72 in 2024 and are down another 33% in the first 9 months of 2025. This is all in spite of higher energy production numbers over those years.

Chevron’s been taking several moves to improve its situation. First, the company started executing a plan in late 2024 to save $2 – 3 billion in structural costs by the end of 2026. Chevron also went shopping and closed on the acquisition of Hess Corporation this past July. However, the acquisition was financed by the company issuing another 300 million shares of stock, increasing the number of outstanding shares by 17%. The company also took the first step to establishing a commercial-scale domestic lithium business with the acquisition of 125,000 acres of land with large lithium deposits.

Although Chevron continues to execute cost-savings measures, company revenues will be primarily driven by the price of oil and natural gas. Until those recover, earnings will remain depressed and, with it, dividend growth as well. Last year’s 5% dividend boost was below the company’s 6% compounded growth rate over the last 5 years. I expect this year’s boost to be lower still, at 3 – 4%.

Prediction: 2.9 – 4.1% increase to $7.04 - $7.12

Predicted Forward Yield: 4.70 – 4.75%

Kimberly-Clark Corporation (KMB) – 52 years

The household and personal products company has a large stable of brands known throughout the United States and internationally. Kleenex, Huggies, Scott, Cottonelle, Poise, and Viva are only some of the brands the company is known for. Kimberly-Clark has a long history – it was founded in 1872 and has grown its dividend more than half a century, qualifying as a Dividend King. But like many large companies that get complacent, until recently earnings growth had stalled, falling from $6.87 a share in 2020 to around $5.50 a year from 2021 through 2023. In response, in 2024 Kimberly-Clark announced a series of cost-cutting measures and an associated reorganization, resulting in the company reporting results through three geographic business segments: North America (NA), International Personal Care (IPC) and International Family Care and Professional (IFP). Of the three, the NA segment provides about 55% of total company sales, while IPC and IFP provide about 30% and 15% of total company sales, respectively.

As you might imagine, the company’s international sales are impacted by currency effects, which are quite sizeable. While NA sales grew about 0.2%, sales in the IPC and IFC segments fell in dollar terms due to currency impacts despite volume growth. The net effect was a 2% drop in net sales. However, with the cost cutting initiative, both GAAP EPS and adjusted EPS grew by double-digits, with adjusted EPS up 11% for the full year. The cost cutting impact may be short lived, however as adjusted EPS are down 2.2% in the first three quarters of 2025.

Kimberly-Clark’s management continues to try to transform the company to keep sales growing. The company announced at the beginning of November that it intended to buy Johnson & Johnson spinoff Kenvue in a transaction valued at nearly $50 billion. This will join Kenvue’s health brands – including Listerine and Tylenol with Kimberly-Clark’s. But it is also likely to cause some uncertainty in the near term, or at least until the sales growth and cost savings are banked. So despite the good growth in 2024 and small pullback so far in 2025, I expect Kimberly-Clark to continue its modest dividend growth, which has historically been in the 3 – 4% range.

Prediction: 3.2 – 4.0% increase to $5.20 - $5.24

Predicted Forward Yield: 5.16 – 5.20%

And here are my predictions for 14 other long-term dividend growth companies which should announce annual increases in January:

Company # Yrs Industry Prediction (%) New Annual Rate
Archer-Daniels-Midland Company (ADM) 50 Consumer Defensive - Farm Products 1.0% - 2.9% $2.06 - $2.10
Agricultural supplier ADM has been having a rough couple of years. Adjusted EPS were down 32% in 2024 despite the company repurchasing 35 million shares over the course of the year. And despite announcing cost savings measures of between $500 and $750 million, the company recently revised adjusted EPS guidance downward, with expectations of another 30% drop this year. With EPS continuing to fall, investors will see another year of 4-cent annual dividend growth. Predicted Forward Yield: 3.58 - 3.65%
Air Products and Chemicals, Inc. (APD) 43 Materials - Specialty Chemicals 0.6% - 1.7% $7.20 - $7.28
The industrial gases company took a massive pre-tax charge due to its decision to exit multiple projects primarily related to the company’s U.S. operations. This resulted in an EPS swing from $17.24 in fiscal 2024 to a loss of $1.74 in fiscal 2025. Even adjusting for these one-time charges, non-GAAP EPS were still down 3%. And while adjusted EPS are expected to grow 8% in fiscal 2026, it still won’t take EPS back to where it was in 2024. Air Products announced a 1% dividend boost last year and I think that’s what we’ll see again this year. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.93 - 2.96%
Apogee Enterprises, Inc. (APOG) 14 Industrials - Building Products & Equipment 1.9% - 3.8% $1.06 - $1.08
Apogee provides high-performance glass and other architectural building products for construction projects. The company’s seen earnings slow since 2022 due to lower business, and it looks like the business slowdown is accelerating, with tariffs starting to bite – EPS are expected to be down more than 20% in fiscal 2026. Apogee has made a concerted effort to reduce its share count, buying back more than 16% of all shares since 2020. But with the expected drop in EPS, investors can expect the share buybacks to slow and for the company to announce a third year of 4-cent annual dividend growth. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.81 - 2.86%
Booz Allen Hamilton Holding Company (BAH) 14 Industrials - Consulting Services 3.6% - 6.4% $2.28 - $2.34
The consulting company relies on growing its contracts – which are nearly exclusively with the Federal Government – to keep revenues and earnings growing. As government budgets grew out of control over the last several years, the company and its investors benefited, with BAH compounding its payout by 14% over the last 5 years. Good growth continued in fiscal 2025, with revenues up 12% and adjusted EPS up 15% for the full year. But it looks like the administration’s focus on how federal agency budgets are being used is taking its toll. BAH lowered its guidance for fiscal 2026 and is now expecting a 5% decrease in revenue and a 13% decrease in adjusted EPS. With free cash flow expected to drop as well, investors will see dividend growth continue to slow. I’m expecting an increase in the mid-single digit percentages. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.69 - 2.76%
BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) 15 Financial - Asset Management 2.1% - 3.0% $21.28 - $21.46
Dividend growth has slowed at the asset manager over the last several years, with payout increases below 3% since 2022, well below the 10% compounded growth rate over the last decade. BlackRock continues to grow its assets under management, hitting a new high of $13.5 trillion at the end of September. But between higher expenses and the issuance of additional shares to acquire other companies, including private equity firm HPS Investment Partners, EPS growth is under pressure. I expect BlackRock to announce another small increase in the 2 – 3% range. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.96 - 1.97%
Church & Dwight Co. Inc. (CHD) 29 Consumer Defensive - Household & Personal Products 3.3% - 5.1% $1.22 - $1.24
This consumer products company is a leading producer of baking soda, which forms the base for several of its products. Church & Dwight’s leading brand is Arm & Hammer baking soda, used in baking, laundering, cat litter, toothpaste, and household cleaning. The company also owns the Trojan, Nair, OxiClean, and vitafusion brands, among others. Church & Dwight’s businesses are generally slow growing and the dividend growth rate reflects this – with 7 straight years of 4-cent annual growth, the company’s dividend growth rate comes in at 4 – 5%. The company had a good 2024, with organic sales up 4.6% and adjusted EPS up more than 8%. But with 2025’s numbers expected to be lower – the company is guiding to adjusted EPS growth of 1 – 2% – I think investors will see an 8th year of 4-cent annual growth with a very small chance of something a little larger. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.43 - 1.46%
Cincinnati Financial Corporation (CINF) 65 Financial - Insurance, Property & Casualty 6.9% - 8.0% $3.72 - $3.76
The property and casualty insurer has a good dividend growth rate, consistently boosting its dividend at 6 – 8% each year. It also has one of the longest dividend growth records and is in the middle of its 7th decade of dividend growth. Investors can look forward to continued income growth from this well-managed company. 2024 was a banner year, with premium income up 12% and net EPS up 25% to $14.53. However, new business is slowing this year and net EPS has fallen 9% in the first three quarters. But with the company’s payout ratio a low 22%, Cincinnati Financial is in a good position to reward investors with another boost in the 7% range. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.26 - 2.28%
Comcast Corporation (CMCSA) 17 Communication Services - Telecom Services 7.6% - 9.1% $1.42 - $1.44
The telecommunications and entertainment company has a good dividend growth record. In each of the last six years Comcast has announced an 8-cent annual dividend increase, giving it a 7.6% compounded growth rate over that time, powered by good earnings growth. 2024 was another good year; on an adjusted basis, EPS grew by 14%, with good revenue growth from the company’s Peacock streaming service. While growth has slowed to about 3% in the first 9 months of 2025, investors can still expect a good dividend boost from Comcast. I expect something slightly larger than last year’s 6.5% boost. Predicted Forward Yield: 4.81 - 4.88%
Quest Diagnostics Incorporated (DGX) 15 Healthcare - Diagnostics & Research 5.6% - 6.9% $3.38 - $3.42
Earnings at the medical diagnostics and testing company peaked in 2021 at the height of the COVID pandemic, as demand for testing services surged. Since then, earnings fell and remained around $7.60 - $8.00 from 2022 – 2024. Similarly, adjusted EPS growth was only 2.5% in 2024. 2025 is looking better for Quest Diagnostics, as revenues are up 14% over the first half of the year and the company’s guiding to 9% adjusted EPS growth for the full year. Despite the good year Quest is having, I think investors will see a boost slightly below the company’s long-term compounded growth rate of 8%. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.92 - 1.94%
Consolidated Edison, Inc. (ED) 51 Utilities - Regulated Electric 2.4% - 2.9% $3.48 - $3.50
Serving New York City and Northern New Jersey, Con Ed has been investing heavily in clean energy and responding to NYC’s push to get away from the use of natural gas. Like most utilities, the company relies heavily on approved rate increases from municipal authorities, along with customer growth, to keep earnings growing. The rate increases allowed the company to grow adjusted EPS by 6.5% in 2024, with expectations of another 4 – 5% growth in 2025. However, Con Ed’s dividend growth rate has been pretty much stuck in the mid-2% range since 2015, and that’s where I expect this year’s increase to be since the additional free cash flow will go to investments to upgrade the company's electrical network. Predicted Forward Yield:3.52 - 3.54%
Eversource Energy (ES) 27 Utilities - Regulated Electric 5.0% - 5.6% $3.16 - $3.18
With nearly 4.5 million customers across Connecticut, Massachusetts and New Hampshire, Eversource Energy is New England’s largest energy delivery provider. The company recently reaffirmed its expected compounded adjusted EPS growth rate of 5 – 7% and continues to perform to meet those expectations. Eversource grew adjusted EPS by 5.3% in 2024 to $4.57 and provided guidance for 5% more growth in 2025. The company’s 6% dividend growth rate reflects the consistent earnings growth, although last year’s 5.2% boost was below average. I expect that investors can expect another boost in the 5 – 6% range. Predicted Forward Yield: 4.72 - 4.75%
Fastenal Company (FAST) 26 Industrials - Industrial Distribution 4.5% - 8.0% $0.92 - $0.95
Fastenal is a wholesale distributor of industrial and construction supplies. The company’s sales grew 3% in 2024, but with higher costs, earnings remained flat. Business in 2025 has been better, with sales and EPS up 8% in the first 9 months of the year. The company split its stock 2-for-1 in April, reflecting optimism about future price gains, but while Fastenal has a compounded growth rate of 12%, investors are unlikely to see double-digit growth this year. I expect a boost in the mid-to-high single digit percentages. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.22 - 2.29%
Franklin Electric Co., Inc. (FELE) 33 Industrials - Specialty Industrial Machinery 4.7% - 6.6% $1.11 - $1.13
Named for one of America’s founding fathers and the man who, as legend says, discovered electricity when flying a kite in a lightning storm, Franklin Electric manufactures pumps and associated parts to move water and fuel for a variety of industries. The company has built an outstanding dividend growth record, compounding its payout by 11% over the last decade. However, growth has stalled at Franklin Electric, with EPS down 6% year-over-year in 2024. And while the company expects earnings to rebound in 2025, that will only bring EPS back to where it was in 2023 – and that’s only if you ignore the one-time costs for terminating the company’s pension plan. While the company’s payout ratio of 25% and low debt leaves room for a good boost, I think investors will have to wait another year. I expect a boost in the mid-single digits. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.12 - 1.14%
S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) 52 Financial - Financial Data & Stock Exchanges 6.3% - 7.8% $4.08 - $4.14
Dividend growth at the financial intelligence and data company has slowed over the last 5 years, with the 5-year compounded dividend growth rate of 5.6% less than half the 11.5% rate over the last decade. The company is in the middle of restructuring its business – it recently acquired market data company With Intelligence, is planning on divesting its Enterprise Data Management and thinkFolio businesses, and will spin off its Mobility Division into a separate public company. The results from earlier restructuring moves are looking good: revenue and adjusted EPS in 2024 were up 14% and 25% respectively, and S&P Global is expecting to post another 12% gain in adjusted EPS in 2025. The company is still in the process of buying back stock issued in 2022 to acquire IHS Markit and will use a significant part of its free cash flow for this. Still, I expect dividend growth to accelerate slightly from last year’s 5.5% boost. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.77 - 0.78%

Summary

It was a good month for dividend growth investors. Six of the 26 companies that announced annual boosts came in with double-digit percentage increases, including the two companies I highlighted in my previous article: semiconductor company Broadcom and financial technology company Mastercard. We also saw double-digit boosts from healthcare company Eli Lilly, building products company Owens Corning, specialty chemical company Balchem, recycling and waste management company WM, and hygiene company Ecolab. Most of the other increases were in the low single digits. Notably, widely held biopharmaceutical company Pfizer, with earnings under pressure, skipped its annual dividend increase in December. Pfizer has until the end of 2026 to boost its payout and maintain its status as a dividend growth stock.

Looking forward to January, the dividend increases will be muted. I expect that Chevron’s dividend growth rate will continue to fall as oil prices continue to drop. Slightly larger increases, potentially in the high single-digit percentages, will come from industrial supplier Fastenal, financial company S&P Global and insurer Cincinnati Financial.

As 2025 comes to a close, I am grateful for all the support and kind comments that you’ve provided on my articles. Thank you for spending your valuable time reading the information that I put out and for your comments. I hope everyone has a wonderful New Year season and a profitable 2026!

data.title: Financials Hit All-Time Highs, Why IYF Looks Attractive Heading Into 2026
data.description:
Financials’ relative strength lagged starting around the beginning of the second half, but the group’s alpha turned on the end-of-year afterburners just recently. An M&A boom, a solid year for IPOs, intense corporate debt underwriting, and favorable interest rate trends have all been tailwinds. And, as of now, it doesn’t appear that those industry-specific and macro factors will abate.

Hence, I am sticking with a buy rating on the iShares US Financials ETF (IYF) as we head into 2026. The fund’s valuations are generally attractive, while IYF’s technical situation is very strong. Momentum is just about the best of the 11 S&P 500 sectors, as Financials hovers at all-time highs. Since my Q2 upgrade, IYF has returned 15%, dividends included, on par with the S&P 500’s return.

IYF Leading the S&P 500 ETF YTD

According to the issuer, IYF seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of U.S. equities in the financial sector. It owns shares in U.S. banks, insurers, and credit card companies and is used to express a sector view within the domestic stock market.

IYF is a medium-sized ETF with $4.2 billion in assets under management as of December 25, 2025. That’s up from just $3.5 billion at the time of my previous analysis. The annual expense ratio is moderate at 38 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is close to that of the S&P 500 at 1.3%.

Share-price momentum has improved quite a bit from the summer, earning the fund an A ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Financials, being a cyclical sector, can be risky at times—just refer to the period around Liberation Day when many bank and small banks suffered.

Liquidity is a strong point, though, with average daily volume north of 400,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at just two basis points, per iShares.

For an update on the portfolio, the 5-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar plots primarily in the upper-left corner of the style box, indicating its best to US large-cap value stocks. Just 6% of the fund is classified as growth, but there is a material 34% of assets housed in US SMID-caps (which adds to its potential economic sensitivity).

Today, the price-to-earnings ratio is quite low at 14.5x. Couple that with a long-term EPS growth rate of 12.0%, and the resulting PEG ratio is compelling at 1.20x.

IYF: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) commands the largest weight, at more than 11.5%. I encourage prospective investors to read my take on Berkshire (a hold rating).

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) is the first of the major SPX firms to report during earnings season, and we are within about three weeks of its reporting date. So, we could see volatility tick up leading into mid-January.

IYF: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, returns are rather subdued now through February. I’d call out that we have seen volatility kick up, at times, during March and April. Often, however, the beginning of the year is not all that eventful.

IYF: Mixed Seasonal Trends

The Technical Take

With shares still inexpensive and the Q4 bank earnings stretch coming closer into view, IYF’s technical situation is strong. Notice in the chart below that shares have been in a protracted uptrend since late 2023. The February-April drop this year was unsettling, but IYF’s snapback was just as impressive. Recently, the ETF notched a double-bottom just above the $120 mark. A subsequent breakout above $128 triggered a near-term measured move price target to $136.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the rise, asserting that the bulls control the primary trend. Now, with a high amount of volume by price below today’s level, any pullbacks should be met with some buying demand. The RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph, meanwhile, confirms the new high in price.

The only blemish is an uptrend resistance line that comes into play near the current level.

IYF: Uptrend Intact, Rising 200dma, $136 Near-Term Target

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on IYF. I see the Financials sector index ETF as attractive on valuation, while the technicals augur for higher prices in 2026.

data.title: Weekly Commentary: Just The Facts
data.description:
For the Week:

The S&P 500 gained 1.4% (up 17.8% y-t-d), and the Dow rose 1.2% (up 14.5%). The Utilities advanced 1.2% (up 13.8%). The Banks rose 1.8% (up 32.1%), and the Broker/Dealers added 1.3% (up 31.0%). The Transports increased 0.5% (up 11.0%). The S&P 400 Midcaps gained 0.7% (up 8.1%), and the small cap Russell 2000 added 0.2% (up 13.6%). The Nasdaq 100 gained 1.2% (up 22.0%). The Semiconductors jumped 2.0% (up 44.7%). The Biotechs increased 0.7% (up 29.6%). With bullion jumping $194, the HUI gold index surged 4.3% (up 171.3%).

Three-month Treasury bill rates ended the week at 3.5425%. Two-year government yields were unchanged at 3.48% (down 76bps y-t-d). Five-year T-note yields were about unchanged at 3.70% (down 69bps). Ten-year Treasury yields slipped two bps to 4.13% (down 44bps). Long bond yields declined three bps to 4.80% (up 1bp). Benchmark Fannie Mae MBS yields fell four bps to 5.04% (down 81bps).

Italian 10-year yields declined three bps to 3.55% (up 3bps y-t-d). Greek 10-year yields dipped two bps to 3.44% (up 23bps). Spain's 10-year yields fell four bps to 3.29% (up 23bps). German bund yields slipped three bps to 2.86% (up 50bps). French yields fell five bps to 3.56% (up 37bps). The French to German 10-year bond spread narrowed about two to 70 bps. U.K. 10-year gilt yields dipped two bps to 4.51% (down 6bps). U.K.'s FTSE equities index slipped 0.3% (up 20.8% y-t-d).

Japan's Nikkei 225 Equities Index jumped 2.5% (up 27.2% y-t-d). Japan's 10-year "JGB" yields added two bps to 2.04% (up 94bps y-t-d). France's CAC40 declined 0.6% (up 9.8%). The German DAX equities index added 0.2% (up 22.3%). Spain's IBEX 35 equities index was unchanged (up 48.1%). Italy's FTSE MIB index slipped 0.3% (up 30.5%). EM equities were mostly higher. Brazil's Bovespa index rallied 1.5% (up 33.8%), and Mexico's Bolsa index jumped 2.6% (up 32.6%). South Korea's Kospi rose 2.7% (up 72.1%). India's Sensex equities index was little changed (up 8.3%). China's Shanghai Exchange Index advanced 1.9% (up 18.3%). Turkey's Borsa Istanbul National 100 index declined 0.4% (up 14.9%).

Total money market fund assets (MMFA) added $7.5 billion to a record $7.673 TN - with a 21-week surge of $597 billion, or 20.7% annualized. MMFA were up $868 billion, or 12.7%, y-o-y - and ballooned a historic $3.042 TN, or 66%, since October 26, 2022.

Freddie Mac 30-year fixed mortgage rates declined three bps to 6.18% (down 67bps y-o-y). Fifteen-year rates rose three bps to 5.50% (down 50bps). Bankrate's survey of jumbo mortgage borrowing costs had 30-year fixed rates down five bps to 6.50% (down 83bps).

Currency Watch:

December 22 - Bloomberg (Erica Yokoyama and Takashi Umekawa): "Japan has a 'free hand' to take bold action against currency moves that are not in line with fundamentals, Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said, in her strongest warning yet to speculators following the yen's weakening even after a rise in interest rates. 'The moves were clearly not in line with fundamentals but rather speculative,' Katayama said… 'Against such movements, we have made clear that we will take bold action, as stated in the Japan-US finance ministers' joint statement,' she said."

For the week, the U.S. Dollar Index declined 0.7% to 98.022 (down 9.6% y-t-d). On the upside, the South Korean won increased 2.5%, the Australian dollar 1.6%, the New Zealand dollar 1.4%, the Norwegian krone 1.3%, the Swedish krona 1.2%, the Canadian dollar 1.0%, the British pound 0.9%, the Swiss franc 0.8%, the Japanese yen 0.8%, the Mexican peso 0.7%, the South African rand 0.7%, the Singapore dollar 0.7%, the euro 0.5%, and the Brazilian real 0.3%. China's (onshore) renminbi gained 0.51% versus the dollar (up 4.2% y-t-d).

Commodities Watch:

December 26 - Bloomberg (Robin Paxton, Preeti Soni and Yvonne Yue Li): "Gold, silver and platinum jumped to all-time highs to extend a historic end-of-year rally for precious metals, with support from escalating geopolitical tensions, US dollar weakness and thin market liquidity. Spot gold rose as much as 1.6% to a peak above $4,540 an ounce on Friday. Spot silver for immediate delivery advanced for a fifth session, climbing as much as 7.6% to cross $77 an ounce. Frictions in Venezuela, where the US has blockaded oil tankers and ramped up pressure on the government of Nicolás Maduro, have added to the precious metal's haven appeal. Washington also launched a military strike against Islamic State in Nigeria in collaboration with the African nation's government."

December 23 - Bloomberg (Mark Burton and James Attwood): "Copper prices topped $12,000 a ton for the first time, extending the metal's recent bull run as mine outages add to concerns about supply of the vital industrial metal. Benchmark prices climbed as much as 2% on the London Metal Exchange on Tuesday to $12,159.50. The metal has gained more than 35% this year and is on track for its biggest annual jump since 2009."

The Bloomberg Commodities Index jumped 3.4% (up 13.9% y-t-d). Spot Gold rose 4.5% to a record $4,533 (up 72.7%). Silver surged 18.0% to $79.2708 (up 174.3%). WTI crude inched eight cents higher, or 0.1%, to $56.74 (down 21%). Gasoline dipped 0.6% (down 16%), while Natural Gas rallied 9.6% to $4.366 (up 21%). Copper jumped 6.0% (up 45%). Wheat gained 1.8% (down 6%), and Corn rose 1.4% (down 2%). Bitcoin declined $750, or 0.8%, to $87,320 (down 6.8%).

Market Instability Watch:

December 24 - Bloomberg (Mia Glass): "Japan's two-year government bond yield rose after an auction of the tenor drew weak demand amid speculation that the Bank of Japan may need to raise interest rates more aggressively to rein in inflation and support the yen. The bid-to-cover ratio… was at 3.26, compared with 3.53 at the previous sale and a 12-month average of 3.65. The two-year yield jumped 2.5 bps to 1.125%, its highest level since 1996."

December 22 - Bloomberg (Edward Bolingbroke): "Traders are loading up on Treasury options targeting a bond rally that would send US 10-year yields back to 4% in coming weeks, a level not seen since the end of November. CME… data… confirmed heavy buying in one March 10-year options contract over the past week. The total premium paid on the position so far is unusually large at approximately $80 million, and open interest, or new positioning held by traders, has soared to 171,153 options - a 300% rise in a week."

December 23 - Bloomberg (Maria Elena Vizcaino): "DoubleLine is warning of a 'brewing collision' in some developed economies as governments from France to the UK and Japan struggle to balance social demands against ballooning fiscal deficits. Political jolts and runaway budgets, which have long plagued emerging markets, are now factoring into investment decisions in the developed world, according to Bill Campbell… at Jeffrey Gundlach's firm… Campbell warned of a 'vicious loop' that has played out time and again in developing economies. Embedded benefits and rising fiscal costs hurt the outlook for growth and public spending, leading to currency depreciation and rising rates, which in turn fuel social tensions and political instability. 'Until recently, the developed markets had been largely immune to such doom loops," he wrote. 'But the tide has changed'."

U.S. Credit Trouble Watch:

December 21 - Wall Street Journal (Matt Wirz and Heather Gillers): "Wall Street fund managers want 401(k) plans to include private credit, but similar products they have already sold to individual investors are in sharp decline this year. Some of the same money managers leading the charge to 'democratize' private markets-like KKR and BlackRock-are among the worst performers in the publicly traded private-credit funds called business development companies. Business development companies, or BDCs, typically make high-interest loans to midsize corporations with junk credit ratings, using income from the loans to pay big dividends to their investors. They have become a popular way for fund managers to draw mom-and-pop investors into the booming private-credit industry. Demand for BDCs surged and the cash they manage has more than tripled since 2020 to about $450 billion… Now, a number of BDCs have stumbled."

December 19 - Bloomberg (Rene Ismail): "The default rate across 1,200 US private debt borrowers tracked by Fitch Ratings increased to 5.7% in November from 5.2% in October on a trailing 12-month basis… The default rate rose to 9.3% in November from 7.7% in October for a subset of 300 privately issued loans rated by Fitch… About 59% of the 91 defaults from November 2024 through last month stemmed from payment-in-kind or interest deferrals, while bankruptcies made up just 7%."

December 22 - Financial Times (Sujeet Indap and Akila Quinio): "Private credit firms snapped up nearly 14 times as much consumer debt this year as in 2024, piling into riskier areas such as credit cards and buy now, pay later debt. In 2025, private credit groups, including the likes of KKR, Blue Owl and Sixth Street, either purchased or struck so-called forward flow agreements to purchase $136bn of consumer loans, according to… KBW analysts. That number compared with just $10bn in the previous year. The rush of deals… raises concerns about underwriting standards and risk management by Wall Street firms that are quickly expanding their empires. 'These deals underscore an emerging trend where private capital is fuelling rapid growth in unsecured consumer lending, while regulated incumbents continue to move with caution,' the KBW analysts said."

December 25 - Wall Street Journal (AnnaMaria Andriotis): "Goldman Sachs has been trying to clean up a mess inside its publicly traded private-lending company for more than three years. Investors are still unimpressed. The bank's business-development company, Goldman Sachs, is primarily a lender to middle-market corporations. Like all BDCs, it raises funds by selling shares, or taking on its own debt, and lends it out in bespoke private-credit deals, with the income paid out in dividends. Those dividends have made BDCs a popular investment for individuals. Thanks to souring loans, the per share value of Goldman BDC's holdings has slid for seven straight quarters and its stock price has fallen even further."

December 24 - Bloomberg (Scott Carpenter): "Tricolor Holdings founder Daniel Chu collected nearly $30 million in compensation in the year leading up to the subprime auto lender's collapse amid alleged fraud, according to a lawsuit… Chu 'defrauded Tricolor by using corporate funds to pay for lavish personal expenses and by forcing the company into paying him tens of millions of dollars in bonuses (on top of his executive salary),' trustee Anne Burns said… That compensation was 'premised on his ability to deliver exceptional financial results - results that were the product of the fraud'."

December 22 - Financial Times (Sujeet Indap): "JPMorgan… was aware for more than a year of 'serious material weaknesses in accounting practices' at Tricolor Holdings, the… subprime auto lender and retailer that collapsed in September, according to a civil lawsuit from its bankruptcy trustee. According to the complaint filed by Anne Elizabeth Burns, the court-appointed trustee managing the Tricolor estate, a JPMorgan audit in February 2024 'revealed approximately $13 million in double pledged loans on collateral pledged to JPMorgan'."

December 23 - Bloomberg (Reshmi Basu and Eliza Ronalds-Hannon): "Saks Global Enterprises, facing limited options ahead of a more than $100 million debt payment due at the end of this month, is considering Chapter 11 bankruptcy as a last resort… Saks raised billions of dollars from bond investors late last year to finance a bold turnaround plan centered on the acquisition of Neiman Marcus, betting that scale would revive the struggling luxury retailer. Instead, the deal deepened the company's debt burden…"

Global Credit and Financial Bubble Watch:

December 23 - Bloomberg (Gerson Freitas Jr, Abraham Gonzalez Dominguez, and Rainier Harris): "Companies across the US and Europe are preparing to sell a record amount of high-grade bonds in 2026, testing investors' appetite as yields drift lower. Morgan Stanley strategists predict more than $2 trillion in US investment-grade debt sales to hit the market next year, which would be the most ever. That's expected to be driven by AI expansion projects, refinancing of looming maturities and acquisition financing. The upbeat sales forecast comes despite questions on how much longer the current credit boom can run."

December 22 - Financial Times (Kate Duguid): "US companies have sold $1.7tn of investment-grade bonds in 2025, a near-record sum stoked by a rush of borrowing to fund artificial intelligence infrastructure that has spurred concerns over a debt glut. This year's issuance - closing in on the $1.8tn issued in 2020 as businesses rushed to shore up their finances during the Covid pandemic - has come as companies took advantage of relatively low borrowing costs to refinance their debt. But the debt sales… also reflect an AI borrowing boom, as Big Tech groups including Meta, Alphabet, Amazon and Oracle tapped bond markets to fund data centres and the energy systems needed to power them. AI-related borrowing now accounts for about 30% of net investment-grade issuance, according to Goldman Sachs, and is expected to grow in 2026…"

December 20 - Bloomberg (Josh Saul and Gerson Freitas Jr.): "The great artificial intelligence boom that's fueling US economic growth now depends heavily on credit markets to finance the investments, and utilities are among the key borrowers… Bond sales by US utilities grew 19% this year to a record $158 billion, funding rampant growth in power demand driven by the artificial-intelligence boom. A lot more is coming: electric companies are expected to spend more than $1.1 trillion on power plants, substations, and other grid infrastructure over the next five years, according to… Edison Electric Institute, up about 44% from the previous period."

Trump Administration Watch:

December 22 - Bloomberg (Katanga Johnson, Patrick Clark, Olga Kharif, Ari Natter and Allison McNeely): "President Donald Trump moves fast. He signed 26 executive orders within hours of being sworn in for the second time - more than any modern president and roughly triple the number signed by his predecessor on his first day. The more-than 140 orders in his first 100 days touched everything from a wind farm project in Idaho to a cryptocurrency issuer in New York. Agencies were told to clear backlogs, unwind rules and speed up approvals. Investors got the message. Pipeline projects were revived and renewable-energy projects canceled. China Watch:

Locally pioneered electric cars zip past deserted apartment blocks. Factory robots run by artificial intelligence churn out products that jobless college graduates cannot afford. State technology funds throw billions of dollars at money-losing startups even as the national debt surges to unprecedented levels. The emergence of AI startup DeepSeek earlier this year showed China can challenge the U.S. in some of the world's most competitive technologies. But Beijing's gains are coming at a steep cost, with the state's heavy-handedness in directing investments wasting colossal amounts of money."

December 22 - Bloomberg: "China Vanke Co., once the country's biggest developer before it succumbed to an unprecedented property crisis, won last-minute support from creditors to extend a bond grace period in a reprieve that helps it avoid default, at least for now."

December 23 - Bloomberg (Alan Wong): "S&P Global Ratings downgraded China Vanke Co. to selective default…, labeling the developer's recent onshore bond extension as a distressed debt restructuring tantamount to a default."

December 22 - Wall Street Journal: "China Vanke, once the country's largest home developer, is no longer too big to fail. As the state-backed property giant buckles under the weight of its debt, the government has so far refrained from stepping in. Analysts say that sends a clear message: Beijing isn't coming to the sector's rescue. Vanke, which has about $170.43 billion in assets, is set to become the latest domino to fall after the Shenzhen government abruptly reversed its position on a partial bailout of the developer. Many of China's other large developers have already defaulted, and a Vanke collapse would raise questions about how policymakers plan to address the real-estate slump as it drags on into a sixth year in 2026."

December 21 - Reuters (Jiaxing Li): "Investors are snapping up yuan credits and surging yuan lending is poised to overtake overseas dollar loans at Chinese banks as attractive pricing helps drive a sustained push by Beijing to put the yuan on the global stage. China's overseas bank lending has tripled in four years to 2.52 trillion yuan and sales of onshore and offshore yuan debt are at or near records for the second year running."

Europe Watch:

December 23 - Associated Press: "France's fractured parliament approved an emergency bill… designed to prevent a U.S.-style government shutdown next week, after negotiations on a 2026 budget collapsed. With just days left before the new year, President Emmanuel Macron and his Cabinet met Monday night to present the brief draft law. It aims 'to ensure the continuity of national life and the functioning of public services,' including collecting taxes and disbursing them to local authorities based on tax and spending levels in the 2025 budget…"

December 23 - Bloomberg (Giovanna Coi): "Home prices in Portugal posted another record jump in the third quarter, with the deepening housing squeeze fast becoming one of the country's biggest political flashpoints. The average price for a dwelling rose 17.7% from a year earlier... It's the biggest increase since the agency began tracking the data in 2009, and the third record in a row. Existing homes drove much of the surge in the third quarter, with prices climbing 19.1% year on year - outpacing the broader market."

Japan Watch:

December 25 - Bloomberg (Erica Yokoyama and Akemi Terukina): "Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's government plans to unveil a record initial budget for the fiscal year starting in April, increasing outlays at a faster pace than inflation. The budget for the year beginning April 2026 will total about ¥122.3 trillion ($786bn)… That represents an increase of roughly 6.3% from the ¥115.2 trillion allocated for the current fiscal year, marking the largest initial budget on record."

December 25 - Wall Street Journal (Megumi Fujikawa): "The Japanese cabinet… approved a record-high initial budget totaling approximately $785 billion for the coming fiscal year, signaling Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's latest effort to support growth through aggressive spending… It includes an 8.8 trillion yen defense allocation and increased interest payments. The prime minister's slogan of 'responsible and proactive fiscal policy' has sparked concerns over public finances, driving bond yields higher. Coupled with expectations for further interest-rate hikes by the Bank of Japan, the benchmark 10-year Japanese government bond yield rose to a nearly 27-year high of 2.1% this week."

December 25 - Bloomberg (Komaki Ito and Yoshiaki Nohara): "Japan's industry ministry is set to nearly quadruple its budgeted support for cutting-edge semiconductors and artificial intelligence development to about ¥1.23 trillion ($7.9bn) for the fiscal year starting in April. Overall the Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry's budget rose by about 50% from the previous year to ¥3.07 trillion, largely due to the jump in chips and AI spending. After Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's cabinet signed off on…"

December 25 - Wall Street Journal: "Bank of Japan Gov. Kazuo Ueda said… the central bank is getting closer to achieving its 2% inflation target, reaffirming his stance of seeking further interest-rate increases. 'Amid tightening labor market conditions, firms' wage- and price-setting behavior has changed significantly in recent years, and the achievement of the 2% price stability target, accompanied by wage increases, is steadily approaching,' Ueda said… The BOJ governor expressed confidence in the country's wage-price dynamics, noting that 'the likelihood of Japan's economy returning to a so-called zero-norm state, in which wages and prices hardly change, seems to have decreased considerably'."

December 24 - Bloomberg (Toru Fujioka): "Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda signaled further interest hikes are likely coming next year, by projecting rising confidence that the central bank is closer to attaining its sustainable price target. 'The achievement of the 2% price stability target, accompanied by wage increases, is steadily approaching,' Ueda said… 'Amid tightening labor market conditions, business behavior has shifted significantly on setting wages and prices in recent years'."

December 25 - Bloomberg (Hidenori Yamanaka): "Japan plans to reduce sales of government bonds during the fiscal year starting in April, focusing on cuts to super-long debt. Total issuance of government bonds to institutional investors through auctions will be ¥168.5 trillion ($1.1 trillion), a reduction of ¥3.8 trillion compared with the initial plan for the previous fiscal year… Combined sales of 20-year, 30-year, and 40-year bonds will drop ¥7.2 trillion to ¥17.4 trillion, with the issuance of super-long-term debt falling to its lowest level since 2009…"

December 23 - Bloomberg (Takashi Umekawa): "Japan's Finance Ministry will set a key rate used to calculate the country's likely interest payments on bonds next fiscal year at 3.0%, the highest in nearly three decades… The interest rate underpinning debt servicing costs in the year beginning April 2026 will rise from an initial 2.6% set during August's budget request stage… The level marks a sharp stepping up from 2% used in the current fiscal year and would be the highest since fiscal 1997, likely pushing up Japan's debt servicing to a new record."

Leveraged Speculation Watch:

December 25 - Wall Street Journal (Gunjan Banerji): "Family offices are the new power players on Wall Street. A growing number of wealthy Americans are launching family offices, firms that do everything from investing money for the superrich to managing their personal affairs. They are huge and secretive, and their influence on Wall Street and Main Street is only growing. Families with these offices recently oversaw about $5.5 trillion in wealth, a 67% jump from five years ago, according to Deloitte. The firm expects that figure to rise to $6.9 trillion this year and top $9 trillion by 2030. It estimates that in coming years, these offices will manage more money than hedge-fund firms. Banks and other firms are hungry to cater to family offices' every need… 'It's not just growing, it's exploding,' said Hendrik Jordaan, a partner at Nelson Mullins who works exclusively with family offices. 'I really think about the family office world being the next private equity'."

December 21 - Financial Times (Costas Mourselas and Amelia Pollard): "Macro hedge funds are enjoying their best year since at least 2008, as huge swings in the price of currencies, commodities and bonds have provided fertile conditions for traders. An index from data provider HFR tracking the returns of such funds - which aim to profit from economic trends by trading equities, bonds and commodities - was up 16% at the end of November, putting the sector on course for its most profitable year in data stretching back to 2008. Hedge funds such as Andrew Law's Caxton and Chris Rokos's RCM have enjoyed returns which were well into the double digits this year…"

Social, Political, Environmental and Cybersecurity Instability Watch:

December 21 - Washington Post (Naftali Bendavid): "The covid.gov website says covid-19 most likely originated in a Chinese lab, although scientists are in fact deeply divided over its origin. A widely read report posted on energy.gov/topics/climate concludes that humans' impact on climate is relatively small, a finding sharply at odds with the scientific consensus. On DHS.gov, the government informs Americans that nearly 2 million undocumented migrants have 'self-deported' this year, an assertion that mystifies researchers. And cdc.gov/vaccine-safety dismisses the conclusion that vaccines do not cause autism as not 'evidence-based.' Researchers and activists increasingly fear that under the Trump administration, the U.S. government is abdicating its historic role as a clearinghouse for reliable information - a momentous shift for what has been the world's foremost producer of widely accepted data for everyone, including academic researchers, local governments and ordinary citizens."
