data.title: The 2025 Metal Frenzy: A Year-End Wrap-Up And 2026 Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

It's been a wild ride for markets throughout 2026. Most catalysts have revolved around the Trump Administration's tariff madness, geopolitical conflicts, the endangered Federal Reserve's independence, the pursuit of an AI frenzy, and the setup of the debasement trade.

And one particular asset class has dominated all five of these major yearly themes - a crossroads of multiple factors that have targeted precious metals. Even with most traders taking their breaks, platinum (+10%!), gold (+1.50%), and silver (+6.50%) are all reaching new all-time highs in today's session.

What helped metals to get where they are?

The first factor is probably the key to most of the others – US President Trump took office on January 20 and, following his program to the letter, began to announce new international policies that prompted a significant shift in the US Dollar.

Central banks have amplified an already begun massive diversification trend after 15 years of global US dollar asset and currency domination. The People's Bank of China continued accumulating massive amounts of gold as Beijing prepared for yet another trade war, leading a wave of influx into the yellow metal.

Gold had already rallied quite extensively as markets were pricing the end of hiking cycles in 2022 towards asset-boosting cuts, rising from $1,600 to over $4,500 in three years.

But Gold Wasn't the Central Target

However, gold wasn't the main target in 2025 – the fact that it sustained its elevated prices even as the Federal Reserve pushed back against rate cuts led to a metals market-wide frenzy.

At the beginning of April, Liberation Day had already triggered a first wave of purchases of the precious commodity, as global policies were forced to become less dependent on the now irrational United States policy. Stock markets marked a bottom, allowing the AI trend to flourish yet again.

The market had been volatile but relatively calm until August 2025, when the regime-changing Jackson Hole speech from Powell occurred.

The Post Jackson Hole Regime Change

After the symposium, however, markets concluded that things would not be the same anymore:

The political pressure to cut rates will be harsh, tariffs will put upward pressure on prices yet again, and the Fed's independence will be even more challenged, prompting immediate fury towards silver, which then spread to other metals such as platinum, palladium, aluminum, and copper.

The rally in metals naturally spread to related equities like Barrick Mining Corp. (B), First Majestic Silver Corp. (AG), and Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), which have all rallied considerably.

Exacerbated by immense investments in data centers, power cells, and all the wires and technologies required to create the AI world we know today, metals saw market-changing and non-stop demand, consolidating without retracing much.

Following a calm period in October, the pricing of the December rate cut propelled all metals to all-time highs, and the momentum has continued, even with the ongoing holiday trading.

All primary precious metals have either reached or surpassed multi-year highs, with some, if not most, even setting new all-time records. As 2025 comes to an end, it will be interesting to see what 2026 has in store for the markets.

What to watch for in the metals market in 2026

Central Bank diversification is the first one. Rate cuts are still priced and expected in the US, prompting a further boost as non-yielding assets flourish in the environment.

The US dollar and US treasuries still represent the significant holdings of global central banks, and American equities are the dominant attractors of capital. Hence, any outflows in such would provide a fundamental underpinning to metal prices.

Geopolitical turmoil may not subside significantly, and metals represent strong safe havens in such an environment.

Even as conflicts are abating, more are emerging around with the ongoing US-Venezuela tensions, more minor regional conflicts, such as Thailand and Cambodia or even the not-seemingly stopping Ukraine-Russia conflict (both nations are huge suppliers of precious metals), but the worst are those that haven't made their way to the headlines, such as China's tensions with Japan and Taiwan and Iran potentially making its way to more headlines.

Government debt isn't expected to subside soon, as the new geopolitical regime could exacerbate already high levels of debt, further boosting metals.

But things will surely not be a straight line to record highs.

However, some counterarguments could arise. Are prices overextended compared to the current use and fundamental value of the metals? An eternal question in markets. However, looking at the 2025 yearly close, it doesn't appear that demand is stalling for now.

On the other hand, what could make the difference would be if alternative metals begin to squeeze as harshly as silver and gold. Platinum and palladium have begun a catch-up move to their peers. However, metals that still have some room to rally could be copper and aluminum, which are widely used in electronic gear and electricity production.

Are the bottlenecks from huge investments into AI still going to be as big in 2026 as supply tries to catch up to the demand? AI spending may not increase, but it will likely continue at least through 2026 and should maintain a high demand bar in the coming years.

Will potential unseen inflation be a booster or a diminishing factor for metal prices? Inflation, by itself, boosts metal prices as the relative purchasing power of fiat currencies decreases. However, if another wave forms on the surface and central banks start hiking again, metals might experience some stalling.

For example, with the Bank of Japan adopting a progressively more hawkish (yet hesitant) tone, there will be less "cheap" funding for the commodities. Don't forget the Swiss National Bank, which took its rates to 0% in June 2025. It is expected to maintain its rates low for now.

An unexpected global slowdown could also slow the demand for tech centers, which would imply a lesser demand for industrial metals.

Despite the volatility in geopolitics and trade, consumption and GDP growth continued on a strong upward path in 2025. With market bubble fears and slowing hiring, economies may experience at least a slowdown, with a tail risk for the worst.

Predicting future flows is a daunting, multi-billion-dollar task where the best in the industry attempt to provide their views on end-2026 prices. I will provide my attempt at forecasts for the final 2026 prices, along with a bearish and bullish case, to offer hypothetical price ranges for primary traded metals.

The Bullish Case for Metals – Fiat Debasement and Government Deficit continues

  • Gold $5,500
  • Silver $80
  • Platinum $3,000
  • Palladium $2,750
  • Copper (per pound): $7.50

The Bearish Case – Global Demand Slows down as Governments back off their High Spending

  • Gold: $3,500
  • Silver: $45
  • Platinum: $1,800
  • Palladium: $1,700
  • Copper: $5.00

Even the most bearish scenarios would result in maintaining the current elevated pricing, as government debt and risks remain elevated. AI and technologies should remain top spending directions for global firms, with electricity production taking the metals trade to the next phase.

Things are subject to significant change as markets are very unpredictable, and the picture will draw progressively as months go by. The rest will be for the future. One thing is for sure: few could have predicted such an erratic year for markets in 2025.

Happy New Year celebrations and a final week of the year.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: LQDH: Rate-Hedged Investment-Grade Corporate Bond ETF, Solid Yield And Performance Track-Record
data.description:
I recently wrote an article on the iShares Interest Rate Hedged Long-Term Corporate Bond ETF (IGBH), which invests in investment-grade long-term corporate bonds, and hedges its rate risk with swaps. The iShares Interest Rate Hedged Corporate Bond ETF (LQDH) is a similar ETF, but focusing on medium-term investment-grade bonds.

Both are incredibly similar ETFs, with extremely similar characteristics, fundamentals, and performance. I'm bullish on both, due to their above-average dividend yields, good performance track-records, and somewhat reduced risk and volatility. I would expect IGBH to marginally outperform, as it's a bit cheaper, with a 0.14% expense ratio versus 0.24%, and as longer-term bonds tend to yield more than medium-term investments. As the differences are tiny, I'm sticking with a buy rating for LQDH, but still marginally prefer IGBH.

LQDH - Overview and Analysis

Strategy and Portfolio

LQDH is an interest rate hedged corporate bond ETF.

LQDH's corporate bond exposure consists of an investment in the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), the largest investment-grade corporate bond index ETF in the market, and the industry benchmark. LQD is a well-diversified fund, with investments in over 3,000 securities from most relevant industries. Credit quality is good, albeit with relatively few investments in the highest-quality assets with the highest ratings.

LQDH uses interest rate swaps to hedge out its rate risk or exposure. In simple terms, these swaps are profitable when rates increase, and the profits cancel out bond price losses from higher rates. On the flip side, the swaps are unprofitable when rates decline, and the losses here cancel out any bond price gains from lower rates. Lots of math to make sure everything adds up in the end, but the end result should be effectively zero rate risk, and duration figures confirm this.

Due to the above, LQDH should see very low losses when rates increase. As an example, LQDH's share price was down 2.7% from 2022 to 2024, during which rates sky-rocketed. Although there were small losses, these were much smaller than those of LQD, the fund's unhedged counterpart, with higher total returns as well.

Conversely, price gains should be very small when rates decline. As an example, rates have slightly declined YTD, resulting in LQDH's price being up 0.6%, compared to 3.6% for LQD. Both price and total returns compare unfavorably to those of LQD.

Due to the vagaries of option prices and premiums, as well as ETF distribution requirements, LQDH's price and its distributions might move in unexpected ways moving forward. As an example, the fund might be forced into distributing swap profits to shareholders during a future period of rising rates. LQDH's bond portfolio would decline in price, and without any swap profits to buoy the portfolio, share prices would decline. Total returns shouldn't be impacted in any way though.

As an aside, I imagine some readers noticed the above explanation and analysis is quite similar to that of IGBH. Both are incredibly similar ETFs, so the analysis was bound to be incredibly similar too.

Risk and Volatility Analysis

LQDH focuses on high-quality bonds and hedges out its rate risk. Realized volatility should be quite low, as is indeed the case. Take special note of LQD in the graph below.

Drawdowns should be below-average too, but that doesn't seem to be the case, with LQDH suffering a massive +25% drawdown during the pandemic. Losses were comparable to those of high-yield corporate bonds, in spite of LQDH's higher credit quality. At least the recovery was swift, with only 5.5% in losses by late 2020.

LQDH's losses were entirely caused by bearish sentiment and selling pressure caused by the pandemic.

LQDH's losses were higher-than-average, because the fund hedges out its rate risk, and rates tend to plummet during recessions.

In my opinion, it was purely coincidental that the two factors above led LQDH to have similar losses and performance to high-yield corporate bonds during the pandemic. I would expect LQDH to outperform these bonds during any future downturn, although I'm not certain that this will be the case, and results would be ultimately dependent on the specifics of any downturn regardless.

LQDH's rate swaps should prove to be a negative during most future downturns, an important negative for investors to consider. LQDH remains a high-quality fund with negligible duration and below-average volatility.

Dividend and Performance Analysis

LQDH's corporate bonds generate around 4.5% in income, as per LQD's dividend yield, with the fund's swaps generating another 1.6%, for a 6.1% dividend yield. Although the swaps generate some income, don't think of these as income-producing investments, and focus on their rate hedge properties.

LQDH has significantly outperformed most bonds since 2022, due to prior Federal Reserve hikes. It significantly underperformed before 2022, as rates were slashed during the pandemic. It has outperformed since inception, because the relative gains from the hikes outweighed the relative losses from the cuts.

Moving forward, I would expect LQDH to perform similar to its benchmark, LQD. Rate swaps should not generate a significant amount of returns long-term, as swaps are simply not priced for this being the case. Significant short-term outperformance or returns is much more possible, and could result in outperformance for several years, but matching the performance of the benchmark remains the base case scenario.

Other Considerations

LQDH's investment-grade corporate bonds are impacted by, well, investment-grade corporate bond rates, with Federal Reserve rates having a more indirect effect on these. LQDH should underperform its unhedged peers when investment-grade bond rates decline, which might not coincide with periods of declining Federal Reserve rates. As an example, LQDH has outperformed LQD since 2024, even as the Fed has cut rates a few times.

Results are somewhat dependent on the specific timing, with similar returns for these ETFs since mid-2024, a few months before the cuts.

In any case, the cuts during the most recent cycle have not led to LQDH underperforming, future cuts might not either, and I don't believe that they will. Current guidance is for one cut next year, and that is simply too little to meaningfully impact bond prices or, at the very least, too little to overweight other factors (investor sentiment, credit spreads, expectations, etc.). At the same time, bonds seem reasonably well-priced right now, and I believe upcoming cuts are priced-in already. Conditions could always change, but as of right now I expect LQDH to perform in-line with its benchmark in the medium-term.

IGBH Comparison

As mentioned previously, IGBH and LQDH are incredibly similar funds, with similar characteristics, fundamentals, and performance. Comparing their returns since inception shows this quite clearly:

Disregard the price volatility during 2015, that was a temporary spike in the fund's discount.

Although the performance of these funds is incredibly similar, they do have a couple differences.

IGBH is cheaper, with a 0.14% expense ratio compared to 0.24% for LQDH. This is a small advantage for IGBH over LQDH, but an advantage nonetheless.

IGBH focuses on longer-term corporate bonds, which generate more income than medium-term securities. Specifically, IGBH gets its bond exposure through IGLB, which has a higher dividend yield than LQD.

Said income could be hedged out by the interest rate swaps used by these ETFs. I'm not confident that this is the case, but it could be. Both ETFs yield about the same, consistent with this.

In my opinion, and notwithstanding the above, the higher income of IGBH's underlying corporate bonds does benefit the fund, even if that isn't reflected in the fund's dividend yield. Differences in swap premiums, prices, and characteristics, could easily explain the above.

IGBH's longer-term corporate bonds are a bit more impacted by changes in credit conditions relative to LQDH. As an example, a 1% increase in default rates implies a 10% drop in price for a 10y bond, compared to a 1% drop in price for a 1y bond (the math is a bit more complicated, but the overall point remains). Expect higher losses for IGBH during downturns and recessions, as was the case during the pandemic.

Due bear in mind, both ETFs focus on quality, investment-grade corporate bonds, so long-term default rates and losses are quite low. Losses here should prove temporary, as was the case during the pandemic, with both ETFs posting positive returns by late 2020.

Overall, IGBH is a bit more exposed to credit conditions than LQDH. Although this is definitely a negative, and an impactful one during the pandemic, it shouldn't significantly impact the fund's long-term returns.

In my opinion, IGBH's lower expense ratio is the most important consideration here and makes it a slightly superior investment to LQDH. As both ETFs are quite similar, both remain a buy.

Conclusion

LQDH invests in investment-grade corporate bonds and uses swaps to hedge out its rate risk. LQDH's good 6.1% dividend yield, performance track-record, and somewhat reduced volatility, make the fund a buy.
